``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined By statute, there is a continuum of staggered parole-release formulas from 25-percent to 75-percent mark based on offense severity and criminal history. (For crime committed after July 1, 2023, sentences for designated serious crimes have been made non-parolable.) The state also uses an “administrative parole release” system that streamlines the procedural path to release compared with traditional parole frameworks. A large percentage of people in prison in South Dakota are assigned “initial parole dates” shortly after admission with the expectation that they will be released without a hearing before the parole board if they comply with the requirements of their “individual program directives.” Discharge credits could total as much as 270 days per year advancing mandatory release 43 percent.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility Year In 2020, there were 34 people in prison past their parole eligibility year. This group made up 2% of people in prison for new crimes and with sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Demographics The demographics of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveal Data on race and ethnicity is incomplete or missing. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 18-24 years old. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Criminal Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: In 2020, 76% of people in prison past their parole eligibility year were in prison for non-violent offenses. The breakdown of criminal offenses of people in prison past their parole eligibility year reveals a varied landscape, with the majority of people incarcerated for property (38%) and drugs (29%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Original Sentence Lengths In 2020, among the prison population eligible for parole but not yet released, people with sentences between 2 to 4.9 years constituted the majority, representing 50%. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Missing Data This section will contain information related to missingness in the NCRP data. Keep?


# Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 2%, changing from 3,434 in 2010 to 3,353 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: Source: BJS Prisoners Series

# Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 10%, dropping from 3,253 in 2010 to 2,929 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 74% of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 74% increase compared to 2015. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics of People Released The demographics of people released from prison reveal Data on race and ethnicity is incomplete or missing. Gender distribution indicates a predominance of males over females. Age-wise, the majority of people were 25-34 years and 35-44 years old. These findings provide insights into the populations transitioning back into the community. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Time Served by Offense Between 2010 and 2020, there were shifts in the average time served by individuals for different offense types in South Dakota. The largest change was for other violent offenses offenses, which increased by 109%. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
# Disparities ## Incarceration Rates In South Dakota, Black, non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 7.5 times higher and Hispanic, any race people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.7 times higher and Other race(s), non-Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 6.3 times higher than White, non-Hispanic people, when accounting for population sizes in the community. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
